original_abstract:
The two-stage time series design is a fundamental tool in environmental epidemiology. In recent years, this framework has been enhanced by the introduction of distributed lag non-linear models (DLNMs)—which simultaneously capture non-linear and delayed effects—and multivariate meta-analysis, which allows for the pooling of complex, multi-parameter associations. However, combining these two powerful methods in multi-location studies has remained a challenge; the high-dimensional nature of DLNMs often results in too many parameters for standard meta-analytical models to handle computationally.

In this paper, we propose a novel methodological development to bridge this gap. We describe a technique to reduce high-dimensional DLNM estimates into simpler, one-dimensional summaries—such as overall cumulative exposure-response curves or lag-specific associations. These summaries are expressed via a reduced set of parameters that remain compatible with multivariate meta-analysis while preserving the flexibility of the original model. 

We demonstrate the utility of this approach using a 10-region case study of temperature-related mortality in England and Wales. Our results show that this method allows researchers to bypass the restrictive assumptions or simplifications (such as using simple moving averages) often required in previous multi-city studies. We provide the implementation of this framework through the R packages *dlnm* and *mvmeta*, along with reproducible code. This approach enables the synthesis of complex environmental health associations across different populations without sacrificing the rich temporal and non-linear detail provided by DLNMs.
humanized_abstract:
The two-stage time series design is a cornerstone of environmental epidemiology. In the last decade, distributed lag non-linear models (DLNMs) have become widely used as they allow to model at the same time non-linearity and lag effects. Meanwhile, multivariate meta-analysis (MVM) has been introduced as a sophisticated tool to combine multi-parameter associations. However, there remains a need for better summarising multi-location analyses, as the MVM of DLNM estimates is statistically and computationally challenged due to the large number of parameters involved.

The previously mentioned methodological developments aim at closing this gap. In this paper, we develop a new methodology that allows to translate high-dimensional DLNM into simpler one dimensional summaries, for example, the overall cumulative exposure–response relation or the lag specific exposure–response relation. These can be formulated through a reduced set of parameters that are compatible with multivariate meta-analysis while keeping the full flexibility of the original models.

Using this method, we illustrate a 10-region case study of temperature-related mortality in England and Wales. The advantages of the method are illustrated in the accompanying R code using the dlnm and mvmeta packages. Our application of the method brings together detailed, non-linear, temporally varied estimates of the association between temperature and mortality at the level of individual urban populations, in a manner that circumvents assumptions and simplifications, such as the use of simple moving averages, that are generally adopted in multi-city analyses.

The previously mentioned methodological developments aim at closing this gap. document.input:
The two-stage time series design is a fundamental tool in environmental epidemiology. In recent years, this framework has been enhanced by the introduction of distributed lag non-linear models (DLNMs)—which simultaneously capture non-linear and delayed effects—and multivariate meta-analysis, which allows for the pooling of complex, multi-parameter associations. In this paper, we propose a novel methodological development to bridge this gap. Our results show that this method allows researchers to bypass the restrictive assumptions or simplifications (such as using simple moving averages) often required in previous multi-city studies.
